coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive and selective phthalazine derivative based fluorescent organic nanosheets for simultaneous detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Wakshe, Saubai B.
Dongare, Pravin R.
Gore, Anil H.
Mote, Gurunath V.
Salunkhe, Shilpa Y.
Mahanwar, Sunanda T.
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: A phthalazine derivative 13-(4-bromophenyl)-3,3-dimethyl-3,4-dihydro-1H-indazolo [1,2-b] phthalazine-1,6,11(2H,13H)-trione based fluorescent organic nanosheets (IDP NS) were prepared by a simple reprecipitation method in an aqueous medium. It can display turn-off type fluorescence with high selectivity and sensitivity for simultaneous recognition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ among other biologically important cations and anions. Under the selected optimized conditions, the fluorescent intensity of IDP NS studied at an excitation wavelength of λex = 360 nm was quenched gradually by the continuous addition of concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching experimental data found to fit the Stern–Volmer relationship in the concentration range of 0–50 μg.mL− 1 and 0–80 μg.mL− 1 with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.0715 µM and 0.0739 µM towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ respectively. This method based on fluorescence quenching was successfully used for the quantitative determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in different environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: IDP NS
Chromium ion
Fluorescence quenching
Selective and Sensitive

Fluorescence studies of IDP NS to cations and anions
Fluorescence titration of IDP NS with Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions solution
Application of IDP NS for Cr6+ and Mn7+ analysis in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Department of Chemistry; Shivaji University, Kolhapur, India for providing the all necessary infrastructural and instrumental facility. The author SBW thanks to Director, Directorate of Technical Education, Government of Maharashtra for granting permission for Ph.D. research work. This work was also supported by the Brain Pool Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (BP Grant No.: 2019H1D3A2A01057526).

This work was also supported by the Brain Pool Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (BP Grant No.: 2019H1D3A2A01057526).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of IDP NS (1 × 10− 4 M) in the presence of different cations and anion (10 μg.mL− 1) in an aqueous solution.
Fluorescence intensity response (ΔF/F0) of IDP NS at λex = 360 nm in the absence and presence of Cr6+ ions (10 µg.mL− 1) and several coexisting ions (10 µg.mL− 1) in an aqueous solution.
Fluorescence intensity response (ΔF/F0) of IDP NS at λex = 360 nm in the absence and presence of Mn7+ ions (10 µg.mL− 1) and several coexisting ions (10 µg.mL− 1) in an aqueous solution.
Fluorescence titration spectra of IDP NS (1 × 10− 4 M) in the presence of different concentration of Cr6+ (0.0–80 µg.mL− 1) in an aqueous solution.
Fluorescence titration spectra of IDP NS (1 × 10− 4 M) in the presence of different concentration of Mn7+ (0.0–80 µg.mL− 1) in an aqueous solution.
Stern–Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of IDP NS by Cr6+ ions in an aqueous solution.
Stern–Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of IDP NS by Mn7+ ions in an aqueous solution.
UV–visible spectral changes of IDP NS (1 × 10− 4 M) with increasing concentrations of Mn7+ in an aqueous solution.
UV–Vis absorption spectrums of IDP NS, IDP NS−Mn7+, IDP NS−Cr6+, and maximum excitation and emission spectrums of IDP NS.

Fluorescence decay profile of IDP NS in absence and presence of different amount of Cr6+ ion solution (Red) in the absence of Cr6+ and (Blue) and (Green) in the presence of 20 and 40 μg.mL− 1 of Cr6+.

Fluorescence decay profile of IDP NS in absence and presence of different amount of Mn7+ ion solution (Blue) in the absence of Mn7+ and (Green) and (Pink) in the presence of 20 and 40 μg.mL− 1 of Mn7+.

Representation of the average particle size and zeta potential of IDP NS in response to the addition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ concentrations in IDP NS suspension (a) in the absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+, and (b) and (c) in the presence of 20 μg.mL− 1 of Cr6+ and Mn7+ respectively.
Determination of Cr6+ in Water Samples via a Standard Addition Method (n = 3).
Determination of Mn7+ in Water Samples via a Standard Addition Method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive and selective phthalazine derivative based fluorescent organic nanosheets for simultaneous detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tarsadia Institute of Chemical Science, Uka Tarsadia University, Bardoli 394350, Gujarat, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Uka Tarsadia University, Bardoli- Mahuva Road, Tarsadi, Gujarat 394 350, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A phthalazine derivative 13-(4-bromophenyl)-3,3-dimethyl-3,4-dihydro-1H-indazolo [1,2-b] phthalazine-1,6,11(2H,13H)-trione based fluorescent organic nanosheets (IDP NS) were prepared by a simple reprecipitation method in an aqueous medium. It can display turn-off type fluorescence with high selectivity and sensitivity for simultaneous recognition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ among other biologically important cations and anions. Under the selected optimized conditions, the fluorescent intensity of IDP NS studied at an excitation wavelength of λex = 360 nm was quenched gradually by the continuous addition of concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching experimental data found to fit the Stern–Volmer relationship in the concentration range of 0–50 μg.mL− 1 and 0–80 μg.mL− 1 with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.0715 µM and 0.0739 µM towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ respectively. This method based on fluorescence quenching was successfully used for the quantitative determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in different environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The design and development of ion receptors for sensing applications have become a significant area of research in supramolecular chemistry [1]. The attention gained by ion receptors is due to the essential role of ions in the fields like environment control, biology, food chemistry, pharmaceutical analysis and clinical diagnosis [2]. Amongst all ions, the identification of cations has gained significant attention from researchers in recent decades because of their biological and environmental importance [3–6]. Chromium usually exists in the trivalent and hexavalent oxidation states in groundwater, seawater and soil [7]. Chromium is an important element in living things for keeping efficient lipid, protein and glucose metabolism [8]. In contrast, Cr6+ is a poisonous ionic species and is a common industrial contaminant. It spreads in an aqueous medium mostly through wastes from textile manufacturing, inorganic chemical plants, leather tanning, electroplating, cooling tower blowdown, etc. [7–10]. It also directly enters into the drinking water supply chain through corrosion-inhibitor commonly used in water pipelines. Hence, even very low concentrations of Cr6+ cause serious health hazards, including dental erosion, renal damage, irritating mucous membranes, generating pulmonary compassion and targeting the respiratory tract and skin [11,12]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and US Environmental Protection Agency also set guidelines for the permissible level of Cr6+ in drinking water is 16 mg.L− 1 and less than 0.1 mg.mL− 1 respectively [13,14].

Similarly, manganese is the most abundant metal in the earth’s crust. It occurs in the environment and our daily nutrients, which meet an average daily intake of about 5 mg/kg [15–17]. Manganese is an essential trace nutrient in all forms of living system with an extensive variety of biologically important roles [18]. Manganese participates in large numbers of biochemical processes in the way of the cofactor in the stimulation of different classes of enzymes and photosynthetic apparatus and is used widely as a versatile tool for biological studies [19,20]. However, any change in the concentration level of manganese can results in disorders of the central nervous system [21] causes serious symptoms like sleep disturbances, fatigability, gait changes, muscular pain, hypertonia, hallucinations and mental irritability, leading to a Manganese-induced Parkinson disease [22]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an accurate and sensitive detection method for the selective determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in an aqueous medium.

Up to now, several modern analytical methods for the determination of Cr+6 and Mn7+ ion have been developed such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [23], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [24,25], Auger-electron spectroscopy [26], electrochemical assays [27] and polarography [28]. All these methods are not only time-consuming but also required sophisticated instrumentation, trained operators. However, in the last two decades, fluorescence chemosensors for the detection of ions pay special attention to the researchers of chemical and environmental sciences owing to their wide range of advantages over other techniques such as high selectivity, sensitivity, ease of detection, rapid response rate, low cost, spatial and temporal resolution [29,30]. Furthermore, in fluorescent chemosensors, the fluorescence properties of the sensor changes with the interaction of a particular analyte. Hence, the change in fluorescence property of the sensor could act as the measurable unit of that specific analyte [31,32].

Typically, the design of a fluorescence chemosensor consists of two integrated parts: one is the signaling fluorophore and the other is the guest receptors that possess a recognizing capability; and are linked through a spacer to form a fluorophore-spacer receptor framework [33]. Thus, when a guest molecule is bind to the receptor, the photophysical properties of the fluorophore such as emission wavelength, fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime change using different sensing mechanisms, and provides valuable information about the presence of that particular guest species [34,35].

In recent times, the rapid development of fluorescent organic nanosheets (NS) and nanoparticles (NPs) has brought more attention to devise ingenious fluorescent sensors owing to their larger surface area, peculiar size-dependent electronic and optical characteristics, and importance in a biological system as well as environmental concerned [36–38]. Nanosheets and nanoparticles are rapidly developing research in the field of cations, anions and biomolecules sensing because of the enormous variety of organic molecules availability, variability and flexibility in the method of preparation, better water solubility and optical properties, aggregation induced enhanced emission (AIE) [39,40]. Organic nanosheets and nanoparticles were prepared using different methods viz. emulsification evaporation, solvent displacement and emulsification diffusion methods [41]. The reprecipitation method is the most popular method for the preparation of organic nanosheets and nanoparticles that contains either self-assembly because of intermolecular forces or encapsulation into an exterior environment which is quite simple, rapid, inexpensive and green method [42]. Therefore, fluorescent organic nanosheets (NS) and nanoparticles (NPs) are likely to be considered fluorescent chemosensors in an aqueous medium.

In this context, it is interesting that the present work reports the synthesis of small organic molecule IDP and further its fluorescent nanosheets prepared by reprecipitation method, which exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity to Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions based on fluorescence quenching.

4-bromobenzaldehyde, phthalahydride and dimedone were procured from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Chloride, nitrate and sulphate salts of different cation (Zn2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mn7+, Li2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Fe3 +, Hg2+, Ba2+); sodium and potassium salts of the anion (CO3 2−, CH3COO−, SO3 2−) and H2O2 were purchased from S. D. Fine-Chem. Ltd. Mumbai, India. All the A. R. grade solvents ethanol, acetonitrile were purchased from Spectrochem, Mumbai, India and used without further purification. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrum of the prepared compound were recorded on a Bruker Avance 300 MHz NMR spectrometer using CDCl3 as solvent and tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. Infrared spectrum recorded on a Shimadzu IR-450 FT-IR spectrophotometer using KBr pallets. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature using Analytik Jena UV-3600 UV–Visible double beam spectrophotometer using quartz cells with a 1.0 cm path length. The fluorescence spectra of synthesized IDP NS were monitored using JASCO (model- FP-8300, Japan) spectrofluorophotometer with 1.0 cm quartz cell. Morphology and size of nanosheets were examined by scanning electron microscope, SEM (JEON-6360 Japan) and particle size distribution of NS was measured by dynamic light scattering on Malvern Zetasizer nano ZS90. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed under ambient conditions (λex = 360 nm and λem = 453 nm) on a Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrophotometer from HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH that uses nanosecond light-emitting diodes (352, 389, 420, 472 and 584 nm) and the decay curves have been monitored by using DataStation v2.5 software available with instrument.

The required compound IDP was synthesized according to the procedure reported by us [43]. To a mixture of 4-bromobenzaldehyde (1 mmol), dimedone (1 mmol) and phthalahydrazide (1 mmol), 50% of aqueous hydrotropic NaPTS solution was added and the resultant mixture was stirred for two hours at room temperature. The progress of the reaction was monitored by a TLC plate using ethyl acetate as a mobile phase. After completion of the reaction, the obtained crude product was filtered, washed with chloroform, and recrystallized from ethanol to give a yellow solid (Scheme 1 ).
13-(4-bromophenyl)-3, 3-dimethyl-3, 4-dihydro-1H-indazolo [1, 2-b] phthalazine 1, 6, 11 (2H, 13H)-trione (IDP); m.p. 266 °C.

IDP NS were prepared by the quick and simple reprecipitation method [36,44]. 5 mL of IDP solution in acetonitrile (1 × 10− 4 M) was rapidly injected by a micro syringe into 20 mL water with continuous stirring for 24 hr using a magnetic stirrer and then it was sonicated for 1 hr at 30 °C. The IDP molecules nucleate and self-assembled into nanoaggregates to give IDP nanosheets in aqueous suspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and photophysical properties of IDP NS

ce:section.ce:para: All stock solutions of cations and anions were prepared at 100 μg.mL− 1 in double distilled water by dissolving the proper amount of metal salts in water and a stock solution of IDP NS was prepared at 1 × 10− 4 M in an aqueous medium. For spectral measurement, test solutions were prepared by adding 1.0 mL (1 × 10− 4 M) of IDP NS into a test tube containing 1.0 mL (100 μg.mL− 1) of individual cations /anions and the resultant mixture was diluted to 10 mL with distilled water, allowed to stand for 5 min at room temperature and then absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λex = 360 nm and λem = 453 nm with a bandwidth of 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence studies of IDP NS to cations and anions
ce:section.ce:para:
The metal recognition properties of IDP NS were studied by measuring changes in their fluorescence intensity in the presence of different biologically and environmentally important cations and anions viz. Zn2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Co2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mn7+, Li2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ba2+, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, SO3 2− and H2O2 by using the fluorescence spectroscopic method in an aqueous medium at an exciting wavelength of 360 nm. The result of studies shows that the emission spectrum of free IDP NS suspension exhibits maximum emission at wavelength 453 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield (ɸ) value of 6.271. With the addition of 10 μg.mL− 1 of different cations to the aqueous solution of IDP NS (1 × 10− 4 M), it was observed that most of the cations induce a bit changes in the fluorescence spectra of IDP NS. However, in the presence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions, the fluorescence spectrum of IDP NS gives significant quenching of fluorescence indicating IDP NS selectively detects Cr6+ and Mn7+ in aqueous media ( Fig. 1
).

Excellent selectivity of the sensor is one of the essential features for cation detections. Hence, to explore the high selectivity of IDP NS for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions, fluorescence competition experiments were carried out in the presence of coexisting cations and anions. For this purpose, an aqueous solution of IDP NS was prepared, aliquots of Cr6+/Mn7+ (10 μg.mL− 1) and all other coexisting ions (10 μg.mL− 1) were added and their emission spectra measured separately at λex = 360 nm and λem = 453 nm. The bar diagram ( Fig. 2 and Fig. 3
) shows fluorescence intensity change △F/F0, where △F = F0 − F, F0 and F are the fluorescence responses of IDP NS in the absence and presence of other coexisting ions. It is seen that little or negligible changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed for IDP NS with Cr6+ in the presence of other coexisting ions except for the Mn7+ ions ( Fig. 2 orange bar).

In the same way, other coexisting ions have not affected the selectivity of IDP NS and Mn7+ ( Fig. 3 pink bar). This result suggesting that IDP NS show a selective response for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions on account of chelation-enhanced fluorescence quenching concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+, and other coexisting ions did not affect the selectivity of IDP NS towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence titration of IDP NS with Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions solution
ce:section.ce:para: To explore further information about sensing characteristics of IDP NS and its practical use for the quantitative determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions. Under the optimized environments, fluorescence spectra of IDP NS aqueous suspension were recorded by varying the concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in the concentration range 0–80 μg.mL− 1. Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 shows, with the progressive addition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ to the solution of IDP NS, the fluorescence intensity of IDP NS was gradually decreased at 453 nm without any spectral shift in the case of Cr6+, while a significant hypsochromic shift from 453 to 423 nm was observed by the gradual addition of Mn7+. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of IDP NS was quenched up to 89.86% and 91.63% (Quenching efficiency = (I 0–I)/I 0 × 100%) with the addition of 80 μg.mL− 1 of Cr6+ and Mn7+ respectively.

Furthermore, investigation of sensing behavior of IDP NS towards different metal ions indicated that coordination of binding sites in IDP NS with Cr6+ or Mn7+ increases the complex formation between the positively charged surface of IDP nanosheets and negatively charged Cr2O7 2− and MnO4 − which reduced the possible radiative emission that originated from IDP NS and introduces the Cr6+ or Mn7+ induced fluorescence quenching in an aqueous media. Besides, hypsochromic fluorescence spectral shift of IDP NS from 453 nm to 423 nm with the continuous addition of Mn7+ is because of reduction in the electron-donating ability of positively charged IDP NS surface upon interaction with electron-deficient Mn7+. Results, decrease in dipole moment around the IDP NS in conjunction with a destabilization of the excited state relative to the ground state leads to an anti-stokes shift in fluorescence spectra.

As it is well known that, in a homogeneous medium the quencher concentration could be obtained from the well-known Stern–Volmer relationship [45].
(1)
F 0
F
=1+
K sv
Q

Where (F0) is the fluorescence intensity of the IDP NS in absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ and (F) is the fluorescence intensity of the IDP NS when Cr6+ and Mn7+ are present at concentration[Q]. KS
V is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 describe the plot of change in fluorescence intensity (F0/F) versus the concentration of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions respectively. The obtained experimental data for Cr6+ and Mn7+ is well fitted in the following empirical equation (2) and (3),
(2)
F 0
F
= 0. 646 x + 1
(3)
F 0
F
= 0. 1358 x + 1

A linear correlation exists between (F0/F) and the concentration of Cr6+ ion in the range of 0 to 50 μg.mL− 1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9925. A similar kind of linear response was found with increasing the concentration of Mn7+ ion from 0 to 80 μg.mL− 1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9958, which could be used to develop a method for the detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ion. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr6+ and Mn7+ is calculated by equation (4),
(4)
LOD =
3 σ
k

Where σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercepts of the regression lines and k is the slope of the calibration graph [46]. Here, the fluorimetric LOD for Cr6+ and Mn7+ is estimated to be 0.0715 µM and 0.0739 µM respectively. Table S1 summarizes the analytical characteristics of the proposed fluorescence method with the previously reported analytical methods and probes followed by their applicability in the determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+.

It can be seen from the fluorescence spectral studies that although the detection emission wavelength for Cr6+ and Mn7+ are almost equal and have a different linear range of detection. Still the individual identification of Cr6+ and Mn7+ using fluorescence method alone is a challenge. However, the IDP NS can selectively differentiate Cr6+ and Mn7+ from other cations and anions using fluorescence turn-off behavior. As shown in the Fig. S9 and Fig. 8 , we can see the influence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ on the absorption property of IDP NS. With the addition of an increasing concentration of Cr6+, there were two UV–Vis absorption peaks observed at 259 nm and 357 nm. An increase of Cr6+ concentration from 0 to 80 μg.mL−1 resulted in a gradually higher UV–Vis absorbance nearby similarity to the Cr6+ absorption spectra ( Fig. S9 ). While, for Mn7+ four UV–Vis absorption peaks observed at 320 nm, 350 nm, 528 nm and 548 nm (Fig. 8). These observations enable IDP NS to distinguish Cr6+ from Mn7+.

To explore the sensing mechanism of IDP NS towards Mn7+ and Cr6+, further experiments were conducted by absorption, fluorescence spectral, Zeta-sizer and fluorescence lifetime measurements. Generally, the quenching of fluorophore in presence of quencher would possibly due to mechanisms such as ions exchange of metal ions [47], generation of non-radiative ground state complexation [48], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [49] or inner filter process [50]. As shown in Fig. 9 , the IDP NS displays a maximum excitation band at 360 nm and two strong upgraded emission bands at 453 nm and 480 nm respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 9 depicts that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of IDP NS−Mn7+ and IDP NS−Cr6+ displays a absorption bands at 320 nm, 528 nm, 548 nm and 259 nm, 357 nm respectively. This considerable spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of IDP NS−Mn7+ and IDP NS−Cr6+ and the excitation and emission spectrum of IDP NS indicates there is the possible involvement of inner filter effect or FRET mechanism to fluorescence quenching of IDP NS [51]. To further confirm possible sensing mechanisms careful observation of Fig. 9, Fig. 1 and Fig. 5 were carried out. From Fig. 9 it was observed that, the broad absorption spectrum of Cr6+ displayed wide and precise overlap with the excitation and emission bands of the IDP NS, indicating the excitation and emission lights were mutually filtered by Cr6+. So, when the excitation light and emission light reached the solution containing IDP NS and Cr6+; Cr6+ was able to absorb most of the light and the fluorescence intensity of the IDP NS decreased as a result of the inner filter effect. However, from Fig. 1 and Fig. 5 it was observed that the fluorescence quenching of Mn7+ is higher as compared to Cr6+ which confirms quenching of IDP NS by Mn7+ involves other dominant sensing mechanisms rather than the inner filter effect.

To realize the interaction between IDP NS and Mn7+, UV–Visible absorption spectra of IDP NS were examined in the absence and presence of Mn7+ ions. As shown in Fig. 8, IDP NS exhibit maximum absorbance at 281 nm was attributed to absorption effective π-π* transition of conjugated phenyl rings and delocalized π electrons. Upon the addition of Mn7+ to the solution of IDP NS, an absorption peak at 320, 528 and 548 nm emerged and it is non-superimpose with IDP NS, which can be ascribed to coordination Mn7+ with an alkene of IDP NS and upon the photo-excitation of the excited-state electrons, it undergoes redox reaction from Mn7+ to Mn4+ by non-radiative photoinduced oxidative electron transfer (PET) reaction [52].

Furthermore, it is well-known that the formation of the ground-state complex does not alter the lifetime of the fluorescent ligand. Thus, the fluorescence lifetime of IDP NS was determined from time-resolved fluorescence lifetime spectra in the presence of different concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ion solution. Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 show the fluorescence decay profile of IDP NS in the presence and absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ion solution. From figures Fig. 10 and Fig. 11, it is seen that decay profile of IDP NS were superimposed with decay profile of IDP NS in the presence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ with an average fluorescence lifetime values of the IDP NS (1.29 ns), IDP NS+Cr6+ (1.26 ns) and IDP NS+Mn7+ (1.30 ns) confirmed that there is ground state non-fluorescent complex of IDP NS with Cr6+/ Mn7+ ion and it is similar to the conditions of static fluorescence quenching mechanism. Likewise, the almost identical average fluorescence lifetime values of IDP NS in the absence and the presence of the Mn7+ could reasonably eliminate the FRET mechanism and confirmed the fluorescence quenching is due to the non-radiative photoinduced oxidative electron transfer process. Hence, the overall sensing mechanism of IDP NS with Cr6+ and Mn7+ was found to be a combination of inner filter effect and non-radiative PET process respectively with static quenching which results into efficient turn-off fluorescence of the IDP NS [52–54].

In addition, for a detailed assessment of the proposed assumption, particle size and zeta potential measurements of the IDP NS were also performed. Particle sizes and Zeta potentials of IDP NS before and after the addition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ are graphically represented in Fig. 12 . The histogram revealed that, the average particle size of IDP NS was found to be 150 nm, on the addition of 20 μg.mL−1 of Cr6+ and Mn7+ it was decreased to 44 and 51 nm respectively, In addition, the zeta potential of the IDP NS also increased from −22.3 mV to −36.4 mV and −55.04 mV, on the addition of 20 μg.mL− 1 of Cr6+ and Mn7+ solution respectively (Fig. 12 b and 12c). This considerable decrease and increase of particle size and zeta potential may be attributed to the surface adsorption via electrostatic interaction of negatively charged Cr2O7 2− and MnO4 − over the IDP NS surface preventing its direct excitation by the non-radiative PET process [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of IDP NS for Cr6+ and Mn7+ analysis in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practical application of IDP NS, the proposed method was successfully applied for the quantitative determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in different water samples collected from the local area of campus. The collected samples were first filtered through Whatmann filter paper No. 41 to remove suspended matter, impurities, etc., then it was boiled for 5 min to remove dissolved gases. After that, water samples are spiked with standard Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions separately at two different concentration levels and then diluted within working linear range and examined directly with the proposed standard addition method. Table 1 and Table 2 summarize the results obtained by the proposed method which are in good agreement with an average recovery of spiked sample. Therefore, IDP NS can be applicable for Cr6+ and Mn7+ determinations in different environmental water samples.

In summary, we have developed a water-soluble Phthalazine Derivative IDP NS based fluorescent nanosensor by a simple and cost-effective reprecipitation method for sequential recognition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in a 100% aqueous medium. The surface morphology and size distribution results showed that the as prepared IDP NS are Nano flakes, feathery type with an average particle size diameter of 150 nm. The formation of IDP NS was also evidenced by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy which specified involvement of H-bonded aggregation through π-stacking effects and strong emission of IDP NS is due to restricted molecular rotations of adjacent molecules. Furthermore, the spectroscopic results indicated that in presence of various biologically important ions only Cr6+ and Mn7+ induce fluorescence quenching of IDP NS with a lower Limit of Detection (LOD) = 0.0715 µM and 0.0739 µM respectively. The selective fluorescence quenching for IDP NS with the addition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ion was further discussed on inner filter effect and non-radiative photoinduced oxidative electron transfer process respectively and it was supported by photo-physical properties. Importantly, the proposed fluorescent IDP NS nanosensor was successfully applied for the quantitative analysis of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in environmental water samples.

Conception and design of study: Saubai B. Wakshe, Pravin R. Dongare, Anil H. Gore, Gurunath V. Mote, Shilpa Y. Salunkhe, Sunanda T. Mahanwar, Prashant V. Anbhule, Govind B. Kolekar. Acquisition of data: Saubai B. Wakshe, Pravin R. Dongare, Anil H. Gore, Govind B. Kolekar. Analysis and interpretation of data: Saubai B. Wakshe, Pravin R. Dongare, Anil H. Gore, Govind B. Kolekar. Drafting the manuscript: Saubai B. Wakshe, Pravin R. Dongare, Anil H. Gore, Gurunath V. Mote, Shilpa, Govind B. Kolekar. Revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content: Saubai B. Wakshe, Pravin R. Dongare, Anil H. Gore, Govind B. Kolekar.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Department of Chemistry; Shivaji University, Kolhapur, India for providing the all necessary infrastructural and instrumental facility. The author SBW thanks to Director, Directorate of Technical Education, Government of Maharashtra for granting permission for Ph.D. research work. This work was also supported by the Brain Pool Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (BP Grant No.: 2019H1D3A2A01057526).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2021.120534.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of chemosensor for Sr2+ using organic nanoparticles: application of sensor in product analysis for oral care
